Canada's Strathcona seeks to raise MEG Energy stake, opposes Cenovus bid
data.description:
Canadian oil and gas producer Strathcona Resources said on Thursday it intends to buy an additional 5% stake in rival MEG Energy and vote against the acquisition of MEG by another rival, Cenovus Energy .

Cenovus agreed in August to acquire MEG in a C$7.9 billion ($5.72 billion) cash-and-stock deal after MEG's board rejected Strathcona's lower C$6 billion takeover bid in June.

MEG has set October 9 for a shareholder vote on its proposed deal, which its board has approved but needs support from at least two-thirds of investors to go through. The deal is expected to close early in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The deal would create one of the largest oil sands companies in Canada, combining MEG's Christina Lake oil sands operations in northern Alberta with Cenovus' neighboring assets, for combined oil sands production of over 720,000 barrels per day.

Strathcona, which currently holds about 9.2% of MEG, said it will increase its ownership to about 14.2%, strengthening its position as a significant minority shareholder in the company.

Strathcona's executive chair, Adam Waterous, has told Reuters that the company will continue engaging with MEG shareholders before the September 15 tender deadline for its offer.

MEG and Cenovus did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment outside regular business hours.

($1 = 1.3817 Canadian dollars)

EMEA Morning Briefing: E.U. to Scrap Duties on U.S. Industrial Goods
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

France consumer spending and CPI, Germany unemployment and CPI; no major corporate trading updates

Opening Call:

European stock futures were mixed after Asia stocks also traded mixed. The dollar strengthened and the yield on the U.S. 10-year Treasury edged higher. Gold and oil fell.

Equities:

European futures were mixed after the S&P 500 and the Dow industrials hit new closing highs following a government report showing surprising economic strength in the U.S. in the second quarter.

The European Union also set out plans to lower tariffs on a range of U.S. products in a move it said should prompt the U.S. to swiftly reduce its levies on the bloc's automotive sector.

Traders will get their latest look at the inflation picture when the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, the personal-consumption expenditures index, is released later in the day.

Jim Baird, chief investment officer at Plante Moran Financial Advisors, said, "The focus remains on the delicate balance between elevated inflation and a softening labor market."

Forex:

The dollar strengthened after Federal Reserve governor Christopher Waller said he now supports a series of interest-rate cuts beginning in September, with the pace of subsequent moves driven by the incoming data.

"It is reasonable to expect the FOMC [Federal Open Market Committee] to cut interest rates by [a quarter of a percentage point] at the Fed meeting in September," said Brian Levitt, global market strategist at Invesco.

"There is little reason for the Fed to be restrictive with the labor market cooling and inflation expectations in the bond market contained."

Bonds:

The U.S. and other major economies have been watching their longer-term borrowing costs shoot higher.

That means investors can get some of the highest yields in several decades - but they also face the headache of lending to major economies as governments pile on ever more debt.

"It really is the same theme playing out across the G-7," said Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management APO, referring to the Group of Seven major economies comprising Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the U.S. and the U.K. "The fiscal situation is serious in essentially all countries, with some nuances."

Energy:

Oil prices fell, pulling back after overnight gains. Broader concerns remain that OPEC+'s production increases and rising output from producers outside the alliance could soon tip the market into a surplus, according to traders.

Although Brent is still trading at a premium for near-term delivery, signaling tight supplies, that premium has narrowed, reflecting softer demand expectations ahead.

"Crude prices remain rangebound, with tariff-led risks to Indian demand for Russian crude being more than offset by expectations for an oversupplied market into the final quarter and beyond," analysts at Saxo said.

Metals:

Gold prices were flat as geopolitical tensions, renewed trade frictions and dented global sentiment have kept investors leaning on the yellow metal as a safe haven, Phillip Nova analyst Priyanka Sachdeva said.

While profit-taking could cap gains for gold prices after the recent rally, broader macro drivers are still supportive, she noted.

-

Iron ore rose as fundamentals were relatively stable. Sentiment strengthened due to China's work plan for the steel industry for 2025-2026, focused on supply-side reform by regulating production capacity and output, said analysts at Nanhua Futures.

Currently, steel demand is weak, but the analysts said they expect limited short-term downside, and prices could be volatile.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Trump Leans on National Security to Justify Next Wave of Tariffs

The Trump administration plans to expand national-security tariffs on steel, aluminum and a variety of other industries in coming months in hopes of redirecting production in these sectors to the U.S. and thwarting potential legal threats in the trade war.

Tariffs on steel and aluminum were expanded this month, covering more than 400 new product lines with 50% levies and increasing compliance costs for companies. Those charges will likely be broadened further, along with expansions of existing tariffs on copper and automotive parts.

Fed's Waller backs a series of interest-rate cuts over the next six months

Federal Reserve governor Christopher Waller on Thursday said he now supports a series of interest-rate cuts beginning in September, with the pace of subsequent moves driven by the incoming data.

Waller had urged the Fed to cut rates at its last meeting in July, and he said he is more sure now that it was the right call.

Eurozone's Economic Outlook Worsens Amid U.S. Tariffs, Domestic Pressures

Confidence in the eurozone's economic outlook fell back as sluggish growth weighed on sentiment, with little hope of a major rebound ahead, surveys of households and business showed.

The European Commission said Thursday that its economic sentiment indicator for the currency area edged down to 95.2 this month from 95.7 in July, thwarting economists' expectations of a slight uptick in sentiment.

E.U. to Scrap Duties on U.S. Industrial Goods as It Scrambles to Soften Auto Tariff Blow

The European Union will move to eliminate all tariffs on U.S. industrial imports and expand access for American farm products, part of an effort to shield European automakers from steeper duties on their exports to the U.S.

The proposals follow a joint statement last week in which Brussels and Washington agreed to cut levies on European autos to 15% from a provisional rate of 27.5%. In return, the EU committed to lowering tariffs on a range of U.S. goods.

Alibaba Earnings Are Coming. AI, Price Wars, and Tariffs Could Leave Their Mark.

When Chinese e-commerce conglomerate Alibaba Group Holding reports earnings Friday, investors will be watching much more than its online retail business.

Often seen as a bellwether for the Chinese economy, Alibaba has seen shares outperform the wider Chinese stock market this year, rising more than 40% compared with the MSCI China index, which is up about 28%. Alibaba was a Barron's stock pick in October last year, due in large part to its cheap valuation and artificial-intelligence ventures.

Cambricon Technologies Warns of Trading Risks After Stock Surges

Chinese artificial-intelligence chip maker Cambricon Technologies issued a warning about trading risks after a surge in its stock price over the past month.

The company's stock price increase has exceeded most of its industry peers' and major indexes, the Beijing-based company said in an exchange filing on late Thursday.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Iron Ore Rises as Sentiment Improves
data.description:
Iron ore futures climbed to around CNY 787 per tonne on Friday, reaching a two-week high as sentiment strengthened amid steady fundamentals and ongoing scrutiny of China’s 2025-2026 steel industry work plan.

Reports indicate Beijing intends to curb steel production this year and next to address persistent overcapacity.

In March, the world’s top steelmaker pledged to restructure its vast industry, though details on the scale and pace of output cuts remain unclear.

Official data showed China’s crude steel production fell 3.1% in the first seven months of the year to 594.47 million tonnes.

Prices also drew support this week after Rio Tinto suspended operations at its massive Simandou project in Guinea following a worker’s death.

The long-delayed project had been expected to begin exports in November, though the company has not confirmed whether the suspension will affect the timeline.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0242 GMT - Aluminum gains in the Asian session, as producers warn of supply constraints in the base metal. Beijing has capped aluminum smelting capacity to rein in power consumption and emissions, say ANZ Research analysts in a note. Prices could be supported by shifting availability of cheap, subsidized smelters in China, they say, citing Australian mining company South32 CEO Graham Kerr. Outside China, high energy prices have posed challenges for producers--South32 confirmed that its Mozal plant in Mozambique will likely be placed on care and maintenance mode in March, ANZ notes. The three-month LME aluminum contract climbs 0.3% to $2,612.50 a ton. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0240 GMT - Iron ore rises in early Asian trading. Fundamentals are relatively stable, with sentiment strengthening due to China's work plan for the steel industry for 2025-2026, focused on supply-side reform by regulating production capacity and output, analysts at Nanhua Futures write in a note. Currently, steel demand is weak, but the analysts expect limited short-term downside, and prices could be volatile. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.6% higher at CNY786.5 a ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0239 GMT - Palm oil prices fall in early Asian trade, tracking weakness in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade overnight. The two oils often move in tandem due to their usage in similar products. Investors are watching U.S.-China talks for potential soybean purchases that may lift prices, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Technically, trade in CPO futures remains cautious given U.S.-China tariff tensions, but long position opportunities may emerge if prices retest 4,455 ringgit/ton. AmInvestment Bank expects CPO futures to find support at 4,421 ringgit a ton and face resistance at 4,485 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for November delivery is 24 ringgit lower at 4,425 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0008 GMT - Gold prices are flat in early Asian trade. Geopolitical tensions, renewed trade frictions and dented global sentiment have kept investors leaning on the yellow metal as a safe haven, Phillip Nova analyst Priyanka Sachdeva says in a research note. While profit-taking could cap gains for gold prices after the recent rally, broader macro drivers are still supportive, she notes. Gold prices will be shaped by incoming U.S. data such as the July PCE print, the analyst says, as they could cement expectations of a Fed rate cut in September. Spot gold is flat at $3,415.35/ oz. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

1923 GMT - Oil futures settle higher after spending much of the session in the red, with the market watching increased strikes in the Russia-Ukraine war just weeks after hopes were raised for peace talks, while prospects for looser oil balances after the high-demand summer season keep buyers cautious. "Traders are glued to that narrow range," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. "It's the classic push and pull-bulls and bears in a stalemate while everyone waits for the next shoe to drop." WTI settles up 0.7% at $64.60 a barrel. Brent rises 0.8% to $67.62 ahead of tomorrow's October contract expiration. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1913 GMT - Live cattle futures take another step away from recent highs, while hogs extend their recovery, as the end of the summer season approaches. The USDA reports net export sales of beef last week were up 34% from the previous period and 40% above the four-week average. Pork did even better, with weekly exports up 53% from the four-week average. CBOT live cattle contract for October closes off 1%, at $2.3693 a pound, while lean hogs rise 0.4% to 94.275 cents a pound. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1908 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures rise as a weekly storage build comes in below expectations, reducing the inventory surplus over the five-year average for a second consecutive week. Natural gas storage rose by 18 Bcf last week, versus the 28 Bcf estimate in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. "With the actual number coming in shy of even the most conservative estimate, today's report was bullish by any standard," Andy Huenefeld of Pinebrook Energy Advisors says in a note. But "with bearish weather looking to persist across the majority of the U.S. for at least the next two weeks, it may be difficult for the market to continue this week's upside momentum." Nymex natural gas settles up 2% at $2.944/mmBtu as October moves to the front of the curve. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1848 GMT - The number of replacement heifers available to enter the dairy herd as milk producing cows has fallen to a 20-year low, and replacements could fall even further, according to CoBank. A report from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange says the national dairy heifer shortage could persist and grow deeper in the next two years, which would limit growth in the milk supply, before a recovery begins in 2027. At the same time, CoBank said, the U.S. is experiencing an historic $10 billion investment in new dairy processing facilities expected to come online through 2027. According to CoBank, the incentive to produce calves destined for the beef supply stems from a contraction in the U.S. cattle supply, which currently stands at a 75-year low, and which has helped drive beef prices to record highs. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1758 GMT [Dow Jones]--Grain futures stage a late session recovery, albeit remaining close to recent lows, as markets face robust crops while global demand looks unable to keep up. The jury is still out on how healthy U.S. corn and soybean crops will be, but few doubt production will be massive. American farmers have the added worry of decreasing Chinese demand due to trade tensions while a deal remains elusive. On the CBOT, corn and wheat rise nearly 1% while soybeans are flat after spending most of the day in the red. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com)

1713 GMT - Higher-than-expected export sales aren't enough to dispel oversupply concerns and soybeans futures extend losses. "Good growing conditions continue in the Midwest with cool and mostly dry weather in the forecast," Price Group's Jack Scoville writes. Moreover, China hasn't been buying much U.S. soybeans and "traders are worried that demand for the new crop will be a lot less this year," Scoville says. "Prices are still higher in Brazil, but China and other buyers are still buying there for political reasons." Soybeans fall 0.7% on the CBOT. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1611 GMT - Gold futures rise on mounting bets of a September U.S. interest-rate cut. Futures are up 0.55% at $3,467.70 a troy ounce. The precious metal has made steady gains since Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech last Friday, in which he opened the door to easing monetary policy, Trade Nation's David Morrison says in a note. Investors are also keeping a close eye on the weaker U.S. dollar, which is currently influencing gold prices to an extent, Morrison says. A weaker dollar is less competitive with gold's safe-haven aspects and makes it cheaper for international investors to buy dollar-denominated bullion. In regards to the dollar, upcoming data releases including core personal consumption expenditure data on Friday should keep the market on alert, Morrison says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1532 GMT - Grains futures are broadly lower, led by wheat. "Russia is still being watched for dry weather that has so far hurt yields and Ukraine is watched for the same reason, and because of the war that could destroy some fields," Jack Scoville of the Price Futures Group says in a note. But while Black Sea prices have been firming as producers hold back sales to compensate for yield losses, "reports from Russia indicate that later weather has improved and that somewhat higher yields than were forecast earlier are now possible." Southern hemisphere crops appear to be good, he adds. CBOT wheat is off 0.6%, corn slips 0.2% and soybeans are down 0.4%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

Philippine Central Bank Appears Close to End of Easing Cycle — Market Talk
data.description: The Philippine central bank appears to be close to the end of its easing cycle, Commerzbank Research analysts write in a note. BSP had a less dovish tone on Thursday compared to previous meetings, they say. BSP Gov. Eli Remolona has signaled scope for one more rate cut this year. Inflation is expected to remain stable and well below the central bank's 2%-4% target range, they say. "It is unlikely to be a constraint on monetary easing for the rest of the year," they add. Commerzbank Research expects the BSP to deliver one more 25 bps rate cut by year-end.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

India's Kotak Mahindra Bank gains as UBS upgrades to 'Buy' on growth outlook
data.description:

** UBS upgrades rating of private lender to "buy" from "neutral", raises PT to 2,450 rupees from 2,300 rupees, implying a 26% upside from last close

data.title: Polls close in Samoa election after ruling party split
data.description:
Samoa held a national election on Friday with the Pacific Island nation's first female prime minister Fiame Naomi Mata'afa seeking to return to office in a race against the long-serving leader she unseated in 2021, and her former party.

Polls closed at 3 p.m. on Friday (0200 GMT) after around 100,000 voters cast ballots to elect 50 lawmakers to parliament.

Fiame was one of the Pacific Islands' few female leaders, and became known for her sceptical stance on China's security ambitions in the region, and support for island nations to unite to resist pressure from outside powers.

She raised the international profile of the nation of 200,000 people by hosting the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting last year, focused on the impact of climate change in the Pacific.

Fiame became Samoa's first woman leader in 2021, winning an election that unseated the incumbent Tuilaepa Sailele Malielegaoi after 22 years.

Tuilaepa refused to accept the 2021 result for several months, stepping down only after the court backed Fiame's makeshift swearing-in ceremony, held in a tent after she was locked out of parliament.

Fiame asked for parliament to be dissolved in June after being unable to gain enough support to pass budget legislation. She was expelled from her political party, Fa'atuatua i le Atua Samoa ua Tasi (FAST), in January in a factional dispute.

FAST led by La’aulialemalietoa Leuatea Polata’ivao Schmidt, Fiame's new Samoa United Party, and Tuilaepa's Human Rights Protection Party (HRRP) are the main contenders to form government.

Samoa United Party is fielding candidates in around half of the seats, electoral commission records showed.

In Apia, the Samoan capital, residents told the Australian Broadcasting Corporation they were looking forward to political stability, and wanted the next government to focus on the economy and jobs.
